_purpose: Orchestrator continuity across Claude Code sessions. The HUMAN talks to ONE AI (the orchestrator); that AI dispatches scope-specific work via the Agent tool. When the human starts a fresh Claude Code session, the new orchestrator MUST read this file early in startup so it knows: what was last being worked on, what's pending, what decisions were just made, what scopes are currently claimed, what to NOT re-do.
_protocol: Update this file at every significant decision/checkpoint. Treat it like a running session journal — newest at the bottom of recent_decisions, oldest pruned. The aggregator script `node scripts/agent-status.js` complements this with per-scope status from data/coordination/sessions/*.json.
_what_vs_why: As of v0.283.x: this file records WHY decisions were made (running journal). `docs/PRIORITIES.md` records WHAT comes next (strict-ranked backlog). Both must be updated when scope changes. Don't put 'next action' here — put it in PRIORITIES.md.

SkyRenderer (src/renderer/sky.rs) is entirely dead code -- never instantiated or ticked anywhere in the tree (verified via a full grep, night-loop cycle 6, 2026-07-01). It computes sky_color/ambient_light/fog_color/sun_intensity from time-of-day + live Weather state, but nothing consumes any of those outputs. The mothership interior + celestial-pass sun lighting is already driven by the real astronomical Earth-Sun vector (src/lib.rs), which is arguably the more correct choice for a ship in orbit. Question for the operator: does SkyRenderer still have an intended role (e.g. a ground/planet-surface exploration mode with an actual visible sky, distinct from the windowless mothership interior), or is it vestigial from an earlier ground-based game-design phase and safe to delete? Did not force-wire it or delete it without a decision -- either action risks real design intent either way.

Chat "Mute Server" button (src/gui/pages/chat.rs, server cog menu) is a TODO stub (found night-loop cycle 9, 2026-07-01). Investigated whether it is self-contained: it is NOT. There is currently no OS-level desktop notification system (no toast/sound) anywhere in the native client, and no per-channel/per-server unread-badge tracking either -- only per-DM unread (ChatDm.unread) exists. So there is nothing for "mute" to actually silence yet; wiring the button to a bare boolean flag with no consumer would look functional while doing nothing, which is worse than leaving the TODO visible. Question for the operator: what should "mute" actually suppress once notification infrastructure exists -- OS toast popups, an in-app sound, the channel-list unread highlight, or some combination -- and should that notification infrastructure be built as its own feature first? Did not force-build a hollow flag or invent the notification system unsupervised.

recent_decisions.decision: SHIPPED v0.624.0 -- the REAL root-cause fixes for two v0.623 visual-verify bugs the operator hit on launch, plus a toolchain-recovery detour. (1) CISTERN TOPS still missing after v0.623: the cause was Mesh::cylinder_capped itself -- BOTH end caps were wound inward (front faces pointing into the cylinder), so under the renderer CCW-front + back-cull convention the caps were culled and every cylinder-shaped machine (cistern, tanks, podium) rendered open-topped. Proved by hand against the side-wall winding (the reference: side fronts point outward). Flipped both caps. (2) CAN-T-CLICK-MACHINES: NOT a v0.622 flow-viz bug (that commit was render-only, never touched the pick path -- v0.623 lock-banner/force-open were partial mitigations). The actual bug: build-mode ENTRY never rebuilt machine_pick (the hit-test volumes), so machines were unclickable until some OTHER edit (nudging a light -> construction_structure_dirty -> rebuild_homestead -> rebuild_machine_objects) repopulated them -- EXACTLY the operator repro `I have to drag a light first, then it updates and they are clickable`. Fix: entry sets construction_structure_dirty, routing the identical rebuild path immediately. (3) The operators MOVEABLE-MAIN-LINES idea = the already-shipped conduit node-graph (Stage 1); the `paths look wrong` is per-edge Manhattan routing -> Stage 2 trunk hierarchy, now flagged as the priority in conduits-node-graph.md with their feedback recorded.

recent_decisions.decision: SHIPPED v0.625.0 -- VIEWPORT DRAG-TO-CONNECT + array-member move, both operator asks after launching v0.624. WIRING: the panel from/to dropdowns were a confusing way to connect machines; wiring is now a 3D gesture -- select a machine, its declared ports (derive_ports) render as coloured handles above it (pipe-legend colours; OUT ports get an outer target ring), click-drag a handle and a rubber-band line follows the cursor (utility colour over a machine with a compatible same-utility port, RED over an incompatible one), release to add_connection (oriented supply to demand). Ports have no authored anchor (default 0,0,0) so handles are spread procedurally in a ring above the body; port_pick is rebuilt with the machines keyed BY ID (placements can skip a machine, so zip-by-index is unsafe). try_pick_port runs before try_pick_machine so a port beats moving the body; only the SELECTED machine ports are grab-able (clean + performant). The dropdown panel stays as a fallback. ARRAY MOVE: the grain tray would not move because it was an ARRAY cell (positioned procedurally, no editable offset). Fix: dragging an array member now explodes its array into direct instances (detach_array_member, ids + positions preserved so nothing jumps), but ONLY on a real drag past the tap threshold, so a mere select never explodes an array.
recent_decisions.why: Operator (v0.624 launch): wiring machines via the left/right panels is confusing; wants it done in the viewport via inlet/outlet handles you drag to a main line or another machine; AND a grain tray would not move. Grounded the exact port/connection/placement model + the conduit-node drag pattern with two parallel Explore agents before implementing, then mirrored the existing ObjectGrab/try_pick_ infra.

recent_decisions.decision: SHIPPED v0.628.0 -- grid S1 first step: PIPES TERMINATE AT PORT NODES. A machine-machine wire now routes to the matching-utility port NODE (the v0.627 sphere+arrow gizmo above the machine) instead of the generic floor anchor -- in rebuild_connection_objects the route endpoints resolve each machine id to its port-node world pos by matching port.utility.id() to the connection kind (from state.port_pick, fresh because rebuild_machine_objects builds port_pick before calling rebuild_connection_objects), falling back to the floor anchor if no matching port. So a water pipe plugs into the water node and the power wire into the power node, and the two leave the machine at different ring points (less overlap). The operator approved the v0.627 gizmo ("looking great, let us proceed") and had asked for cables/pipes to go to the input/output nodes.

Logged a real, scoped follow-up in FEATURES.md: group voice rooms dont yet appear in the voice_channel_list broadcast (only the channels table is enumerated), so you cant see OTHER participants in a group voice rooms roster yet, though join/leave + audio signaling both work. Night-loop cycle 5: shipped v0.645.0, closing out livestreaming backend verification (priority #2). Live-tested the full stream lifecycle against a local relay: stream_start creates the right DB row + broadcast, viewer join/leave, stream_chat send + persistence -- all confirmed correct. Found and fixed a real bug in the process: BUG-043, handle_stream_viewer_leave and handle_stream_stop both persisted the LIVE viewer_keys.len() as viewer_peak, but that count is only ever highest right at a join and decreases from there -- handle_stream_viewer_join never wrote to viewer_peak at all, so by the time a stream actually ends the recorded peak was frequently 0 or far below the true max. Proved live: 2 viewers joined (true peak 2), both left, stream stopped -- old code would have recorded viewer_peak 0. Fixed with an ActiveStream::peak_viewers high-water mark (src/relay/relay.rs) updated via .max() on every join, used instead of the live count when persisting in both the leave and stop handlers. 4 new unit tests (msg_handlers.rs::stream_tests) using the existing fresh_state/block handler-test fixture, proven via a temporary revert-and-retest to actually catch the bug (both regression tests failed against the old code, recording 1 and 0 instead of 2 and 1). Window-glass z-fight fixed by INSETTING the pane 0.05 m (doors still fill to seal). v0.565: auto-open ring is now a constant-width LINE circle via draw_lines_onto (operator's orbit-path idea) instead of a scaling polygon strip. v0.566: clip_end_to_walls fixes mid-span T-junctions -- a wall ending INSIDE another wall's body (not a shared corner) is pulled back to that wall's NEAR face so a thick wall butts instead of spearing through; openings shift by the clip distance; conservative (free ends untouched). 48 ship tests pass incl. new clip + miter geometry tests.
things_pending_or_known_gaps.why: Operator launch-tested the mitred corners + gizmos and reported real regressions (opening frames skewed -- fixed v0.559; gizmo normals; thick wall through) plus answered the swing-door fork (auto/manual model) and suggested the line-ring. Worked the whole wave to done.
things_pending_or_known_gaps.verification: native + relay + lints green every release; geometry locked by unit tests. REMAINING (operator-directed next): (a) MANUAL-door interaction -- a manual door currently can't be opened in-world (it's a wall until the control panel exists), so the door CONTROL PANEL (the game already has an Interactable raycast system to spawn a panel entity into) is the natural next, making manual+locked meaningful. (b) DOOR-CONTENT SYSTEM (premade catalog / custom+stained-glass / real iris petals / revolving) = data-driven multi-PART doors; the big design feature, wants grounding + operator direction. Both await operator steer.

(2) Control panel ACTIONS beyond open/close: lock/unlock, emergency power, hack (operator's original ask) -- v0.567 only does open/close, so a LOCKED manual door with a panel is currently un-openable in-world (prompt honestly says 'door is locked'; unlock is build-mode-only for now). (3) Destructibility Stage 3 -- per-material wall HP/physics; formula was deferred until re-derived against the real 8 materials + an ordering test (a 1mm steel wall blocks, a 1mm paper one you run through).

things_pending_or_known_gaps.decision: DEV-TOOLS backlog (operator asked 'what helper dev tools could we create for AI + human?'). Grounded 12 proposals (ground-editor-tools workflow output). Recommended first trio = the AI+human READ/ACT/ENUMERATE surface: (1) live home JSON introspection snapshot (HomeStructure::to_introspection_json -> debug/home_snapshot.json on rebuild; an AI can finally READ the live home), (2) a construction TEXT-COMMAND console (add_wall/move_corner/... mapping to the same struct mutations the gizmos use; one edit path for AI + human), (3) list_commands/help (the verb registry is enumerable -- the in-app-ops north star). Other proposals: airtightness/seal visualizer, coordinate+cursor HUD, point-to-point measure tape, light-coverage heatmap, wall/room/light inspector point-query, collision/overlap validator, 'what would the AI do' ghost-diff preview, snap-target+dimension-while-dragging readout. how_to_resume: 2. **Read docs/PRIORITIES.md — top of TIER 0 is your next action.**
3. Run `git status` and `git log --oneline -10` to see recent state.
4. Read this file (orchestrator_state.json) — running journal of WHY past decisions were made.
5. Run `node scripts/agent-status.js` for per-scope coordinator-friendly summary.
6. Read docs/STATUS.md and docs/FEATURES.md for the static feature inventory.
7. Check data/coordination/agent_registry.ron for the canonical scope list.
8. THEN start work. End the session by updating PRIORITIES.md (what's next) and appending a decision to this file's `recent_decisions` (why we got here).
